1: Chapter 15 addresses four categories of bodily discharge: (1) chronic male discharge (Leviticus 15:2-15), likely gonorrhea or a similar urogenital condition; (2) ordinary seminal emission (Leviticus 15:16-18); (3) menstruation (Leviticus 15:19-24); (4) irregular/prolonged female discharge (Leviticus 15:25-30). The chapter is addressed to both Moses and Aaron — indicating priestly administration — and covers both males and females with notable structural symmetry.
2: The chronic male discharge (zav from the root zuv, to flow) generates a sustained impure condition, unlike the brief impurity of seminal emission. Zav was likely gonorrhea or another chronic urogenital inflammation. The condition could last for extended periods, keeping the person in a perpetual impurity state. The zav's impurity is among the most communicable in Leviticus — transmitted by touch, contact, sitting, lying, carrying, and even spitting.
3: Two forms of the discharge: flowing (active) or blocked (impeded). Both generate the same impurity status — the condition of zav is not dependent on active flow but on the ongoing bodily state. This comprehensive definition prevents a narrow interpretation that might exempt someone in a temporarily quiescent phase of the condition.
4: Bed and seat contamination: everything the zav lies or sits on becomes unclean. This contact-transmission system is the most comprehensive in Leviticus — the person's impurity extends into the objects they use rather than requiring direct skin contact. The bed and seat represent the person's constant environment; the law's effect is to mark the entire living space of the zav as requiring caution.
5: The standard decontamination procedure for secondary contact: wash clothes, bathe in water, unclean until evening. The graduated response for the zav's contacts is milder than for direct contact with a corpse (which requires a more elaborate purification) but more involved than some other minor impurities. The three-action sequence (wash clothes → bathe → wait) is the standard template for contamination-by-contact throughout Leviticus 11-15.
6: Sitting on a seat previously occupied by the zav generates secondary contact impurity. The transmission chain: zav → seat → person who sits on seat — this mediated impurity (through an object rather than direct contact) is characteristic of Leviticus 15's concern with impurity transmission. The social implications were significant: the zav's household furniture became a source of impurity for all who used it.
7: Touching the zav's body directly generates the same secondary impurity as touching his furniture. The direct body contact produces the same ritual result as the mediated furniture contact — impurity transmission does not require intimacy; casual contact is sufficient. This reflects the seriousness with which chronic discharge was treated as an impurity source.
8: Saliva from the zav transmits impurity — if he spits on a clean person, that person becomes unclean and must wash. This is among the most specific contamination pathways in Leviticus. The inclusion of saliva reflects an understanding that body fluids more generally (not only the discharge itself) can carry impurity from the affected person to others.
9: The saddle (merkav) the zav rides on becomes unclean — extended to any riding equipment. The contamination extends to all surfaces the zav regularly occupies, moving, sitting, and lying. The comprehensive coverage of bed, seat, saddle, and body means there is virtually no surface the zav contacts that does not become a transmission vector.
10: Touching anything that was beneath the zav (on his saddle/seat) generates until-evening impurity; carrying such objects requires clothes-washing. The carrying escalation (carry → worse than touch) mirrors the pattern in Leviticus 11 with animal carcasses. The graduated severity for levels of contact is a consistent Levitical principle.
11: If the zav touches someone without rinsing his hands first, he transmits impurity. The rinsing exception is notable: if the zav rinses his hands before touching, the impurity is not transmitted in the same way. This handwashing provision anticipates later Pharisaic handwashing traditions (Mark 7:1-5) — the concern for ritual hand-cleansing before contact has deep roots in Levitical purity law.
12: Clay vessels touched by the zav must be broken; wooden vessels rinsed. The same clay/wood distinction as in ch. Leviticus 11:33-35 applies here — clay is porous and cannot be fully cleansed; wood is non-porous and cleanable by rinsing. The consistency of this material distinction throughout Leviticus reflects a coherent understanding of how different materials interact with impurity.
13: When the discharge stops, the zav counts seven days to confirm cessation — the same waiting period as the skin-disease quarantine. Then washing in running water (mayim ḥayyim) — the same living water required for major purification. The cessation of the discharge must be confirmed by a seven-day symptom-free period before cleansing begins; this prevents a brief interruption in the discharge from triggering premature cleansing.
14: On the eighth day: two turtledoves or young pigeons for the priest at the sanctuary entrance. The eighth-day offering parallels the leper's cleansing (Leviticus 14:10) and post-birth purification (Leviticus 12:6) — the eighth day as the day of new beginning and covenant re-entry. The bird offering makes the zav's cleansing accessible at the most affordable price point, ensuring no one is permanently excluded from cleansing by poverty.
15: The priest offers one bird as sin offering and one as burnt offering — the same paired offering as in Leviticus 12:8 and Leviticus 14:22. The sin offering addresses the ritual impurity component; the burnt offering expresses full re-dedication. The formula: atonement (kipper) + cleansing (taher) — both aspects must be addressed for complete restoration.
16: Seminal emission generates a brief, mild impurity: the man bathes and is unclean until evening. This is the mildest category in Leviticus 15 — no waiting period, no sacrificial offering, no clothes requirement (though Leviticus 15:17 implies the garment used is washed). The contrast with the chronic discharge of Leviticus 15:2-15 is stark: normal sexual function generates minimal temporary impurity, while chronic discharge generates extensive persistent impurity.
17: Any garment or leather on which there is seminal emission is washed and unclean until evening. The evening reset restores clean status without ceremony. The mild treatment of seminal emission in Leviticus contrasts with some ancient Near Eastern purity systems where nocturnal emissions generated more serious impurity requiring temple avoidance; Israel's law is notably moderate in comparison.
18: Sexual intercourse between a man and woman generates mutual until-evening impurity for both partners. Both bathe and both are unclean until evening. The symmetry — both partners equally affected — is notable. The impurity does not reflect moral censure of marital sexuality; it reflects the general principle that bodily fluid exchanges produce temporary ritual status changes. The brief impurity does not interrupt normal married life in any significant way.
19: The menstruation section: the woman in her niddah (menstrual separation/impurity) is unclean for seven days. Anyone who touches her is unclean until evening. The seven-day period corresponds to the typical length of a menstrual cycle; the law reflects empirical observation of normal female physiology. Niddah comes from a root meaning 'to drive away/be separate' — the term for the menstrual state that combines physical and social separation.
20: The same bed-and-seat contamination logic as for the male discharge (Leviticus 15:4-5) applies to the menstruating woman. Everything she lies or sits on becomes unclean. The structural parallelism between male and female discharge laws in Leviticus 15 is one of the more striking examples of gender symmetry in the Torah — both receive equivalent treatment in terms of impurity transmission and purification procedure.
21: Touching the woman's bed — wash, bathe, unclean until evening. The decontamination procedure is identical to that for touching the zav's bed (Leviticus 15:5). The parallel treatment of male chronic discharge and female menstruation in terms of secondary contact impurity further demonstrates the structural symmetry of the chapter.
22: Touching her seat generates the same secondary impurity. The comprehensive coverage of bed, seat, and direct contact creates the same impurity perimeter around the menstruating woman as around the zav — though for a fixed seven-day period rather than an indefinite one.
23: Whether the object is the bed or anything she sits on — the secondary impurity applies to anything she has used. This comprehensive clause prevents narrow interpretation: even objects not mentioned specifically fall under the principle of contamination by contact with objects the niddah has used.
24: If a man has intercourse with a woman during her menstrual separation and her flow comes upon him, he is unclean for seven days — and every bed he lies on is unclean. This more serious secondary impurity (seven days rather than until evening) for marital intercourse during menstruation reflects the closer and more intimate contact involved. The same prohibition appears in Leviticus 18:19 as an ethical command, not merely a purity regulation.
25: The irregular female discharge — bleeding outside the normal menstrual cycle, whether an extension of the period or bleeding at other times — generates the same impurity as the niddah throughout its duration. This law directly addresses the condition of the woman in the Gospels who had been hemorrhaging for twelve years (Luke 8:43-48; Mark 5:25-34): she would have been in a perpetual state of zav-equivalent impurity for all twelve years, unable to touch anyone without transmitting impurity, unable to enter the sanctuary, socially isolated by her condition.
26: All objects the woman with irregular discharge lies or sits on are unclean throughout the discharge — the same contamination perimeter as for the niddah but with no fixed end date. The perpetual impurity of irregular discharge made it among the most isolating conditions in Lev 15 — unlike the niddah (seven days) or the zav (with a clear cessation point), irregular discharge had no predetermined end.
27: Secondary contact with anything she has used generates until-evening impurity — the same as for the normal niddah and the zav. The milder secondary contact impurity (until evening) maintains proportionality: those who accidentally contact contaminated objects are not subject to the full seven-day impurity of the primary condition.
28: When the discharge stops, the woman counts seven days — the same confirmation period as the zav (Leviticus 15:13). The cessation must be verified before cleansing begins. The woman's seven-day count parallels the man's seven-day count (Leviticus 15:13), maintaining the chapter's structural symmetry between male and female discharge conditions.
29: On the eighth day: two turtledoves or young pigeons for the priest. The identical offering requirement for the woman with irregular discharge and the male zav (Leviticus 15:14) confirms the symmetrical treatment of both conditions. The eighth-day sacrifice is the universal gateway back to full participation in covenant life after major bodily discharge conditions.
30: Sin offering and burnt offering for the woman's cleansing — the same paired offering as for the zav (Leviticus 15:15), the leper (ch. Leviticus 14:22), and the post-birth purification (ch. Leviticus 12:8). The consistent two-bird sin/burnt-offering pair across all these conditions creates a recognizable liturgical signature for re-entry into covenant community after bodily-impurity periods.
31: venahazartem et-bnei Yisrael mitum'atam velo yamutu betum'atam betamam et-mishkani asher betokham: 'You shall keep the people of Israel separate from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst.' The purpose clause of the entire chapter — and arguably of the entire purity system. The problem is YHWH's presence in the tabernacle at the center of the camp: a holy God dwelling among an impure people creates a potentially lethal proximity. The purity laws are the engineering that makes cohabitation of the holy God and impure humanity possible. This verse is the theological key to understanding why Israel needed the entire Levitical system.
32: Summary heading for the male discharge laws: the chronic zav condition and the seminal emission. The summary runs through Leviticus 15:33 and covers all four categories in the chapter.
33: Summary for the female laws: the woman in her niddah, the man or woman with a discharge, and the man who lies with an impure woman. The comprehensive summary closes the discharge-purity legislation and opens back to the Yom Kippur legislation that follows in Leviticus 16 — the mechanism by which all this accumulated impurity is dealt with annually.
